coredata.dc:title: Preparation of blue- and green-emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots from graphite and their application in bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shah, Hameed
Xie, Wenjing
Wang, Yuanyuan
Jia, Xinrui
Nawaz, Asmat
Xin, Qi
Song, Maoyong
Gong, Jian Ru
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the superior photoluminescence property, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been regarded as promising nanomaterials for biological applications such as bioimaging. However, many of the preparation methods are complicated, high cost, eco-unfriendly, and with a low product yield. Here, we demonstrate a novel top-down approach for NGQDs preparation, in which the low cost graphite was used as a precursor, ammonium persulfate as an oxidative molecule and nitrogen source, and H2O2 as an oxidative agent, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone as a solvent and potential functionalizer. Meanwhile, the solvent extraction was applied for the first time to purify NGQDs. The separated NGQDs display green and blue fluorescence, deriving from the difference sizes and nitrogen doped types. The total product yield of NGQDs is calculated to be about 52%, containing 88% of green-emissive NGQDs and 12% of blue-emissive NGQDs. Meanwhile, our NGQDs own low cytotoxicity, and display a good bioimaging performance in the in vitro and in vivo investigation. The synthesis idea in our work might be also applicable to obtain other kinds of quantum dots from the readily obtainable bulk materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped GQDs
Fluorescent nanoagent
Bioimaging
Nanobiomaterial

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and the high-resolution TEM images of (a) NGQD-d and (b) NGQD-w. Particle size distribution of (c) NGQD-d and (d) NGQD-w. AFM images along with profile line analyses of (e) NGQD-d and (f) NGQD-w. (g) XRD patterns of NGQDs and graphite.

(a) FTIR spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w. (b) XPS survey scan spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w. XPS C1s spectra of (c) NGQD-d and (d) NGQD-w. XPS N1s spectra of (e) NGQD-d and (f) NGQD-w. The black and yellow lines in c-f represent the experimental and curve-fitted spectra, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Normalized UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of (a) NGQD-d and (b) NGQD-w. The PL spectra were obtained using the excitation wavelength near the maximum PLE of the sample. (c) Photostability test of NGQDs excited with UV irradiation at 365 nm. The inset images show the photos of NGQD-d and NGQD-w after storing at room temperature for one year.
Cell viability assay of NGQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) after 24 h (a) and 48 h (b) treatment.

Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HUVECs after incubation with NGQD-d: (a) bright field image, (b) cell cytoplasm stained with NGQD-d excited at 488 nm excitation, (c) cell nuclei stained with DRAQ5 at 633 nm excitation, (d) the merged image of NGQD-d and DRAQ5. Confocal fluorescence microscopy imaging of HUVECs after incubation with NGQD-w: (e) bright field image, (f) cell cytoplasm stained image with NGQD-w at 405 nm excitation, (g) cell nuclei stained with DRAQ5 at 633 nm excitation, (h) the merged image of NGQD-w and DRAQ5.
Fluorescence microscopy images of zebrafish (a) without NGQD-d and after incubated with NGQD-d at a concentration of (b) 10 μg mL−1 and (c) 100 μg mL−1.
A proposed procedure for synthesis of NGQDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation of blue- and green-emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots from graphite and their application in bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) Center of Excellence for Nanoscience, CAS Key Laboratory for Nanosystem & Hierarchical Fabrication, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, 11 Beiyitiao Zhongguancun, Beijing 100190, China

CAS Key Laboratory for Nanosystem & Hierarchical Fabrication
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) Center of Excellence for Nanoscience, CAS Key Laboratory for Nanosystem & Hierarchical Fabrication, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, 11 Beiyitiao Zhongguancun, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology and Health Effects

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: J. R. Gong, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) Center of Excellence for Nanoscience, CAS Key Laboratory for Nanosystem & Hierarchical Fabrication, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, 11 Beiyitiao Zhongguancun, Beijing 100190, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) Center of Excellence for Nanoscience
CAS Key Laboratory for Nanosystem & Hierarchical Fabrication
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the superior photoluminescence property, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been regarded as promising nanomaterials for biological applications such as bioimaging. However, many of the preparation methods are complicated, high cost, eco-unfriendly, and with a low product yield. Here, we demonstrate a novel top-down approach for NGQDs preparation, in which the low cost graphite was used as a precursor, ammonium persulfate as an oxidative molecule and nitrogen source, and H2O2 as an oxidative agent, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone as a solvent and potential functionalizer. Meanwhile, the solvent extraction was applied for the first time to purify NGQDs. The separated NGQDs display green and blue fluorescence, deriving from the difference sizes and nitrogen doped types. The total product yield of NGQDs is calculated to be about 52%, containing 88% of green-emissive NGQDs and 12% of blue-emissive NGQDs. Meanwhile, our NGQDs own low cytotoxicity, and display a good bioimaging performance in the in vitro and in vivo investigation. The synthesis idea in our work might be also applicable to obtain other kinds of quantum dots from the readily obtainable bulk materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the zero-dimensional materials derived from two-dimensional graphene, are regarded as rising star photoluminescence (PL) nanomaterials owing to their small size, tunable PL properties originating from quantum confinement, superior photostability, low toxicity and outstanding biocompatibility [1–4]. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms is an effective method to modulate their electronic structure and surface chemical features, leading to tunable multicolor emission [5–7]. Among the dopants, nitrogen is considered as an excellent candidate for heteroatom doping in GQDs because of its comparable atomic size to carbon atom, and its five valence electrons available for forming strong valence bonds with carbon atoms, resulting in an improved fluorescent quantum yield [8]. So far, nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) can be synthesized by bottom-up and top-down approaches. The bottom-up approach mainly includes the carbohydrate dehydration method such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, and thermal pyrolysis of the small organic molecules and biomass [7,9]. In comparison, the top-down synthesized NGQDs without the small-molecule fluorescent impurities can be obtained by oxidative cutting of the N-doped carbon nanotubes, electrochemical treatment of graphene film in the presence of N-containing molecules, solvothermal treatment of graphene oxide using nitrogen-containing solvent, hydrothermal synthesis from the N-doped graphene, and so on [5–7]. However, the existing preparation processes usually involve complicated and time-consuming procedures including strong acidic treatment which would cause additional cost and pollution [10]. In addition, the as-synthesized NGQDs by both top-down and bottom-up methods contain impurities such as excess precursors, salts, and organic solvents, resulting in a large variation in size and chemical structure of NGQDs, which can affect their properties and also limit their further applications. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a novel method for simple, low cost and eco-friendly preparation of NGQDs with a high product yield, meanwhile, it is necessary to purify the as-synthesized NGQDs to obtain NGQDs with a relative narrow distribution of particle size, chemical structures as well as PL emission spectra.

In this work, we developed a novel top-down approach for preparation of NGQDs using graphite as the raw material, ammonium persulfate (AP) and H2O2 as oxidizing agents avoiding the use of strong acids, and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) as the solvent for the exfoliation of graphite. Considering that both the solvent NMP and the oxidizing agent AP contain nitrogen atom, the nitrogen doping can be realized without further adding of the nitrogen source. An efficient dichloromethane (DCM)-water solvent extraction process was then utilized for the first time, which gave rise to two different kinds of fluorescent NGQDs. The NGQDs dispersed in the DCM and water phase have a high total product yield of 52%, displaying green and blue fluorescent property, respectively. The major difference between the two types of NGQDs is derived from size, the nitrogen doped types and the ratio of oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces. These two NGQDs were then applied to in vitro and in vivo imaging, which showed a clear high-contrast luminescence result with low toxicity and good biocompatibility. This work offers a simple, low cost and eco-friendly top-down method for the preparation of NGQDs with superior PL properties and high product yield.

Graphite flake (natural, 325 mesh) was purchased from Alfa Aesar, Thermo Fisher Scientific (UK). N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Ammonium persulphate (AP) was purchased from Zhonglan Industry Co., Ltd. (China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and dichloromethane (DCM) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (China). The water used in the experiments was purified through a water purification system from Millipore S.A.S. (Milli-Q 18.2 MΩ cm−1, Millipore Direct-Q® 3, France).

During the NGQDs preparation process, 0.5 g of graphite powder was mixed with 2.0 g of AP and 18 mL of NMP in a 100 mL beaker and sonicated for 3 h, and the temperature of the reaction was maintained at 50–60 °C. The sample was then treated with 9 mL of H2O2 followed by stirring and heating at 120 °C for 7 h. After that, an extra 9 mL of H2O2 was added to the reaction. Then the sample was placed inside the autoclave in Teflon bottle and incubated at 220 °C for 6 h in an oven for the solvothermal reaction. The obtained sample was cooled to room temperature and filtered, followed by drying overnight in a vacuum oven at 80 °C to evaporate the residual NMP. The sample was then extracted by using DCM and water solvent extraction, and the obtained solutions were dialyzed against ethanol and ultra-pure water, respectively, in a dialysis bag (retain molecular weight = 500 Da) for one week. The NGQDs extracted in the DCM and water phase were denoted as NGQD-d and NGQD-w, respectively. The quantum yields of the products were obtained by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 350 nm) and the absorbance values (at 350 nm) of the NGQDs with the standard quinine sulfate aqueous solution (quantum yield: 54%) as a reference.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using Hitachi HT7700 (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan) and FEI Tecnai G2 F20 U-TWIN (FEI Co., USA) transmission electron microscopes operating at 80 and 200 kV, respectively. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken using a Multimode 8HR (Bruker Corp., USA) atomic force microscope in the tapping mode. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using an X'pert PRO MPD diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands) with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using the Spectrum One Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses of NGQDs were carried out using ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) source. The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed on the UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Japan). The PL spectra and fluorescent quantum yield of NGQDs were recorded using FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and NanoLOG-TCSPC spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon Ltd., USA). For the photostability test, NGQDs were irradiated under a UV light of 365 nm (Model ZF-7 dark-box multi-function UV-lamp analyzer, Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co. Ltd., China) for 30, 60, 120 and 180 min and then the PL spectra were collected.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and ECM-2 culture medium were obtained from ScienCell Research Laboratories (USA). HUVECs were cultured in ECM-2 medium supplemented with 5% fetal bovine serum, endothelial cell growth supplement (50 μg mL−1), and 1% penicillin-streptomycin using a four-chambered Lab-Tek coverglass system (Nalge Nunc International, USA). All cells were incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator until approximately 80% confluence was reached.

The cell viability test was determined by the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8, Dojindo Molecular Technologies) assay. Briefly, the cultured HUVECs were incubated with NGQDs at a series concentrations (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 24 h. Then the cells were washed with the serum-free DMEM twice and incubated with 100 μL of 10% (v/v) CCK-8 in the serum-free DMEM at 37 °C for 4 h. Finally, the optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation: cell viability (%) = [(ODtreated − ODblank) / (ODcontrol − ODblank)] × 100%. ODblank was obtained without the presence of cell and NGQDs. ODcontrol and ODtreated were measured in the absence and presence of NGQDs, respectively. Each experiment was performed three times and the average data was used.

For cell imaging experiments, the aqueous solutions of NGQD-d (20 μg mL−1) and NGQD-w (20 μg mL−1) were prepared and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore Express). The filtered solution was mixed with the culture medium (1000 μL) and added to three wells of the glass slide chamber (the fourth well used as a control in which the HUVECs were grown). After incubation for 2 h, the HUVECs were washed three times with the phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer solution (500 μL each time) and kept in PBS for optical imaging. Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HUVECs after incubation with NGQDs were then obtained using the Zeiss LSM 710 confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss AG, Germany) at the excitation wavelength of 405, 488 and 633 nm for NGQD-w, NGQD-d and DRAQ5 channels, respectively.

AB Wild type (WT) zebrafishes were obtained from the China Zebrafish Resource Center (Wuhan, China) and were raised at 28 °C with a 14 light/10 dark photoperiod. The adults were placed separately in tanks overnight with a male: female 1:1 ratio and released next morning for spawning. Embryos were collected after 4 h post fertilization (4 hpf) and rinsed with fresh fish water to remove any surface residue.

Thirty healthy embryos at 96 hpf were picked and exposed with NGQD-d at different concentrations (10 and 100 μg mL−1) for 48 h. Each concentration group was treated in triplicate and the exposure solution was renewed daily. After exposure, embryos were anesthetized with 0.016% tricaine (Sigma-Aldrich Corp., USA) and then observed using a stereoscopic dissecting microscope (Zeiss Zoom v.16, Carl Zeiss AG, Germany). Fluorescent images were captured at green fluorescent protein GFP channel (Excitation wavelength at 488 nm and emission wavelength at 500–550 nm).

In our work, the graphite was directly used as precursor avoiding the multi-step process for the preparation of precursors such as the graphene oxide, nitrogen-doped graphene, and nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube in the reported NGQDs preparation works. The NGQDs were synthesized by a solvent-assisted sonication, followed by the heating and solvothermal process. The crude NGQDs were filtered, and then purified by solvent extraction with a mixture of DCM and water, finally involving a dialysis process. After purification, the product yield of NGQDs in the DCM and water were calculated to be about 46% and 6%, respectively. The NGQDs extracted in the DCM and water phase were denoted as NGQD-d and NGQD-w, respectively. The morphologies of the as-prepared NGQDs were confirmed by the TEM and AFM. As displayed in Fig. 1a–d, both NGQDs samples are uniform and well-dispersed, and the average lateral size of NGQD-d is about 5 nm which is larger than that of NGQD-w. The high-resolution TEM images (inset of Fig. 1a and b) show the lattice structure of NGQDs with a lattice space of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) planes [11]. AFM images (Fig. 1e and f) show that the thickness of both NGQD-d and NGQD-w samples is 0.5–1.8 nm, indicating that most of the NGQD-d and NGQD-w consist of 1–4 graphene layers [12]. The structure of NGQDs was then confirmed by XRD (Fig. 1g). For the graphite, the sharp peak centered at around 26° corresponds to the (002) planes with a lattice space of 0.334 nm [7]. However, the main diffraction peak in the spectra of both NGQD-d and NGQD-w sample broadened and shifted to around 22 and 23° with a lattice space of 0.393 and 0.377 nm for NGQD-d and NGQD-w, respectively. The broader peak and larger lattice space of NGQD-d and NGQD-w compared to the graphite might be attributed to the less ordered stacking of NGQDs originating from the incorporation of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of NGQDs.

The chemical composition of NGQDs was first characterized by FTIR. Both the FTIR spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w show similar absorption peaks (Fig. 2a), which verifies the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (OH, CO and CO) and nitrogen-containing functional group (CN) on the surfaces of NGQDs. In addition, the NH stretching and bending vibrations at 3240 and 1620 cm−1 can also be found in the spectrum of NGQD-w, indicating the possible existence of amine or amide in this sample. XPS experiments were then performed to further analyze the chemical structures of NGQDs. Fig. 2b shows the XPS survey scan spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w. Three main elements, including C, O and N, were detected in both NGQD-d and NGQD-w samples, however, both the O/C (0.27) and N/C (0.13) ratios of NGQD-w are higher than those of NGQD-d (0.06 and 0.02 for O/C and N/C), indicating that the water phase NGQDs have a higher oxidation degree. The C1s spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w can be fitted into five components: (1) the sp2-hybridized carbon (CC), (2) the sp3-hybridized carbon (CC/CH), (3) the ether and hydroxyl groups (CO) or CN group, (4) the carbonyl group (CO), and (5) the carboxyl or amide group (OCO or OCN) as labeled in Fig. 2c and d [13]. The intensities of peaks related to oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, ether, carboxyl and amide groups) in the C1s spectrum of NGQD-w are much higher compared to the C1s spectrum of NGQD-d, as shown in Fig. 2c and d. Since the sp2-hybridized carbon (CC) is mainly related to the aromatic structure in graphite, the oxygen- or nitrogen-related peaks (i.e., peaks 3–5) to the sp2-hybridized carbon peak ratios of NGQD-w, which reflect the functionalization degree of NGQDs, calculated from the curve-fitting results are higher than those of the NGQD-d, suggesting that more oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups were incorporated on the surface of NGQD-w, thus contributing to their hydrophilic property. In addition, the sp3-hybridized carbon (CC/CH) peak is the major component in the curve-fitted C1s spectrum of NGQD-d, which might be due to the polymerization product of NMP functionalized on the surface of NGQD-d. The major difference between the N1s spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w samples (Fig. 2e and f) is the presence of -NH- peak in the spectrum of NGQD-w, which is related to amine or amide groups on NGQD-w surface, thus also giving rise to the hydrophilia of NGQD-w.

Based on the characterization results, we proposed a procedure for synthesis of NGQDs (Scheme 1 ). First, the graphite was exfoliated by NMP solvent assisted sonication in the presence of AP, and heating. Herein, the NMP as an organic solvent was used to exfoliate graphite by liquid immersion, while the role of AP was to expand and exfoliate graphite, in which the gases decomposed from AP under the heating and sonication first absorb on the surface or edge of graphite and then diffuse and incorporate into the graphite layer. [14–16]. Next, during the heating and solvothermal processes, H2O2 and the undecomposed AP were used for oxidative cutting the exfoliated graphite sheet, in which the oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy or carboxyl groups were introduced onto the surface of the graphite sheet [17,18]. Meanwhile, NMP and AP worked as dopants to introduce nitrogen-containing functional groups, forming amine, amide or aromatic nitrogen groups on the oxidized graphite surface [19]. Besides, organic polymers could also be formed during the solvothermal process due to the polymerization of small organic molecules existed in the system, and adsorbed or covalently bound to the surface of the oxidized nitrogen-doped graphite sheet [20,21]. Last, after removing the large-size graphite sheets and unexfoliated graphite by filtration (membrane filter pore size, 0.22 μm) and centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 15 min), a simple solvent extraction was performed. During the extraction process, the NGQDs functionalized with a larger ratio of the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups and a smaller ratio of the polymer functional groups tend to presence in the water phase, while the NGQDs with a larger ratio of the polymer functional groups and a smaller ratio of the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups are the main components in the DCM phase. After this extraction and the dialysis process, most of the organic polymer absorbed on the surface of NGQDs can be removed. Thus, the residual organic polymer is the one that covalently bound or tightly absorbed on the surface of NGQDs, which cannot be easily removed. And this organic polymer functionalization can not only affect the hydrophilic of NGQDs but also improve the fluorescent quantum yield of NGQDs [22].

The optical property of the GQDs is a key factor which would affect their applications in bioimaging, thus the measurements of optical properties such as UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of NGQDs were further carried out. Fig. 3a and b show the UV–vis absorption, PL excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of NGQD-d and NGQD-w, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQD-d (Fig. 3a) shows a peak at around 202 nm representing the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and an absorption peak at around 290 nm assigning to the n-π* transition of CO or CN bonds.7 The maximum emission of the NGQD-d centers at a wavelength of 505 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm (Fig. 3a), corresponding to the green fluorescent property. Similarly, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQD-w (Fig. 3b) shows absorption peaks that centers at about 210 and 290 nm, corresponding to the π-π* and n-π* transition, respectively. The PL spectrum of NGQD-w (Fig. 3b) presents a maximum emission of 440 nm excited at 350 nm, imparting its blue fluorescent property. The PL properties can be altered by many factors such as quantum size effect, doping effect, and surface functionalization, and so on. To our best knowledge, both nitrogen doping and increase of size can induce a red shift of PL emission [6,23]. However, the emission wavelength of the NGQD-w with higher N doping degree is relatively shorter than that of the NGQD-d with lower nitrogen doping degree. Thus, instead of nitrogen doping, the quantum size effect is regarded as the determinant factor in PL emission of our NGQDs, which can be explained that the emission wavelength of the NGQD-d with larger size is longer than that of the NGQD-w with smaller size as shown in Fig. 1a–d. In addition, the calculated quantum yields for NGQD-d and NGQD-w are 63.8% and 26.2%, respectively, suggesting the promising capability of NGQDs for bioimaging application [24]. The difference in the PL quantum yield might be due to the different surface states considering that polymer functionalization can effectively increase the fluorescence quantum yield of NGQDs [22]. Further photostability test of NGQDs (Fig. 3c) shows that both NGQD-d and NGQD-w retain >90% of initial PL intensity after 180 min exposure at UV irradiation, indicating that the NGQDs are resistant to photobleaching. In addition, the as-prepared NGQDs can be stored at room temperature more than one year without obvious precipitation (centrifugation at 12500 rpm for 30 min) and the change in the PL emission.

Based on the optical property of NGQDs such as the photostability and high quantum yield, the NGQDs are expected to act as fluorescent probes for bioimaging. Since the cytotoxicity is an important factor that needs to be considered for biological applications of NGQDs, the cytotoxicity of NGQDs was then evaluated on HUVECs using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 4a, no significant change of the cell viability was observed for both NGQD-w and NGQD-d when their concentrations increased from 6.25 to 200 μg mL−1 after incubation for 24 h. When the incubation time further increased to 48 h (Fig. 4b), the cell viability of NGQD-d and NGQD-w could still remain higher than 80% at different concentrations. The results indicate that both the NGQD-d and NGQD-w have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, showing the potential for bioimaging. Therefore, both NGQD-d and NGQD-w samples were incubated with HUVECs for in vitro bioimaging. Meanwhile, a commercial fluorescent dye DRAQ5 was used for nuclear staining. Fig. 5 shows the confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HUVECs after incubation with NGQD-d or NGQD-w. The obtained bright field image of HUVECs after incubation with NGQD-d (Fig. 5a) shows the phase contrast image of the cells, and the corresponding fluorescent images (Fig. 5b–d) reveal that the NGQD-d with green fluorescence mainly is located in the cytoplasm, while the nuclei is stained by DRAQ5 with red fluorescence. Similarly, when using NQGD-w for cell imaging, the nuclei exhibits red fluorescence after stained by DRAQ5 and the NGQD-w is also located in the cytoplasm of HUVECs but with blue fluorescence (Fig. 5e–h). These results indicate that both NGQD-d and NGQD-w can be used for bioimaging with little cytotoxicity.

Zebrafish is one of the promising models owing to its rapid embryonic development, optically transparency and high genetic homology to mammals [25,26]. It has been applied to observe the fluorescent imaging and labeling, assess the toxicity and biocompatibility of GQDs [27–31]. To further explore the bioimaging applications of NGQDs, in vivo imaging of NGQDs was then investigated using the zebrafish as a model. The NGQD-d was selected for the in vivo bioimaging testing considering its relatively higher fluorescent quantum yield than that of NGQD-w. A blank control of zebrafish in the absence NGQDs exhibits almost no fluorescent signal (Fig. 6a), indicating a negligible auto-fluorescence of the zebrafish under 488 nm excitation. However, green fluorescence can be detected in some intestinal areas of the zebrafish after swallowing and skin-absorption of NGQD-d at the concentration of 10 μg mL−1 during the incubation process (Fig. 6b) [32,33]. The labeled areas became larger and the eyes and stomach of zebrafish were also labeled with the green fluorescence as the concentration of NGQD-d increased from 10 to 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6c). Collectively, our NGQD-d can be served as fluorescent nanoagent for in vivo bioimaging and may have the potential to use as a tracker for studying more complicated biological effects in zebrafish or other higher animals.

In summary, a novel, simple and eco-friendly solvothermal method was developed for NGQDs preparation by using graphite as the precursor, NMP as a solvent and potential functionalizer, AP as an oxidative molecule and nitrogen source, and H2O2 as an oxidative agent. Subsequently, the DCM-water solvent extraction procedure was used by the first time for purification of the crude NGQDs. The obtained NGQD samples in the DCM and water phase displays the green and blue fluorescent properties, respectively, which is owing to their difference sizes and nitrogen doped types. Based on their superior fluorescent properties, and good biocompatibility, NGQDs can be used for in vitro and in vivo bioimaging applications. The synthesis idea in our work might be also applicable to obtain other kinds of quantum dots from the bulk materials.

Qi Xin conceived and designed the experiments; carried out the material characterizations including the TEM, AFM, XPS, FTIR, the in vitro bioimaging testing of NGQDs; wrote and revised the manuscript. Hameed Shah conceived and designed the experiments; prepared and purified the NGQD samples, performed the PL characterization, prepared the draft manuscript. Wenjing Xie wrote and revised the manuscript, involved in data analysis and figure preparation, gave advice on the NGQDs' purification. Yuanyuan Wang wrote and revised the manuscript, carried out the in vivo bioimaging testing of NGQDs. Xinrui Jia assisted with the NGQDs characterizations. Asmat Nawaz performed the CCK-8 assay. Jian Ru Gong and Maoyong Song conceived and designed the experiments; wrote and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support for this work from the National Basic Research Plan of China (2016YFA0201600), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21422303, 21573049, 21872043, 81602643), Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2142036), and the Knowledge Innovation Program, Youth Innovation Promotion Association, and Special Program of “One Belt One Road” of CAS.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots for biosensing and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Fan, S. Li, F. Yuan, L. Fan, Fluorescent graphene quantum dots for biosensing and bioimaging, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 19773-19789. doi:10.1039/C4RA17131D.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic Dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots
